content.text: Shipping a Claude-powered iOS app to the App Store introduces review requirements that standard apps never encounter. Apple's reviewers test what your app can produce — not just what it typically shows — which means your system prompt design, content labelling, privacy disclosures, and age rating all need to reflect the full range of Claude's possible outputs. This section maps each relevant guideline to the concrete implementation steps it demands.

attrs.alt: App Store review requirements for AI apps: guideline areas, what they require, and implementation steps

content.text: Guideline 1.1: Objectionable Content

Section 1.1 of the App Store Review Guidelines prohibits apps from containing content Apple deems offensive or harmful. For an AI app, this extends to content Claude generates at runtime. Apple reviewers actively probe AI features by typing prompts — your system prompt is the first line of defense.
A strong system prompt scopes Claude to a narrow domain and explicitly names what it must not produce:

let systemPrompt = """
    You are a cooking assistant for a recipe iOS app.
    Only discuss food, ingredients, cooking techniques, and kitchen equipment.
    Do not provide information on any other topic.
    Do not produce content that is violent, sexual, or discriminatory.
    If asked about anything outside cooking, respond: 'I can only help with recipes and cooking.'
    """
This explicit refusal instruction matters because Claude follows it reliably. Domain scoping also makes it harder for a reviewer or user to steer the model off topic with adversarial prompts.
Guideline 1.2: User-Generated Content (UGC) Mechanics

Apple treats AI-generated content that the user triggers as analogous to user-generated content. Section 1.2 requires apps with UGC to provide: a mechanism to report or flag offensive content, a way to block abusive interactions, content filtering, and a way for users to contact you about problematic output.
Implement a report sheet that users can open from any AI response:
The support email in the sheet doubles as the contact mechanism Apple's guideline 1.2 requires. Make sure the same address is in your App Store Connect contact information.
Age Rating: Rate for What the App Can Produce

Apple assigns age ratings based on the content an app is capable of generating, not just what it displays by default. A general-purpose assistant that can discuss violence, mature themes, or strong language when prompted must carry a 17+ rating, even if most sessions are benign.
There are three practical approaches, in order of review-friendliness:
Narrow the system prompt.
A cooking app that can only discuss food gets a 4+ rating. The system prompt's domain restriction directly determines the age rating ceiling.
Rate 17+ and gate with Sign in with Apple.
If your app legitimately needs a wider scope, accept the 17+ rating and require account creation so you can enforce usage policies.
Explain in App Review Notes.
If the model produces mature content only in tightly controlled scenarios, document this explicitly in the App Review notes field so reviewers understand the intended behavior and rating rationale.
Privacy Nutrition Labels for API Calls

When your app sends user-entered text to the Claude API, that text is data collected from the user. Apple's Privacy Nutrition Labels (configured in App Store Connect under App Privacy) require you to declare what categories of data you collect, how they are used, and whether they are linked to the user's identity.
The relevant data types for a Claude-backed app are typically:
Data Type
Applies if...
Linked to user?
User Content (Other)
User types prompts that you send to Claude
Yes, if tied to account; No, if anonymous
Usage Data
You log how often the AI feature is used
Depends on analytics setup
Identifiers
Never — never include device ID or IDFA in the prompt
N/A
To avoid declaring Identifiers, your prompt construction must strip any device- or account-identifying information before the API call. A helper that sanitises the user's text before sending:

The sanitiser does not strip PII patterns (email addresses, phone numbers) by default — that would require regex matching that could produce false positives. Instead, do not collect PII from users in prompts at all: if your app stores the user's email address, do not include it in the text sent to Claude.
Labelling AI-Generated Content in the UI

Apple's Human Interface Guidelines and the broader design section of the review guidelines expect apps to be transparent about automation and AI. Users should be able to distinguish Claude's output from human-written content. A minimal label using SF Symbols is sufficient:
For high-stakes domains — medical, legal, financial — add a disclaimer beneath the label:
Writing Effective App Review Notes

Apple reviewers encounter many AI apps and will probe yours systematically. The App Review Notes field (under the Version Information section in App Store Connect) lets you explain the AI feature before reviewers start testing. Include:
What Claude does:
'This app uses the Anthropic Claude API to generate recipe suggestions based on the user's listed ingredients.'
How output is restricted:
'The system prompt limits Claude to culinary topics only. Attempts to discuss other topics receive a refusal response.'
Age rating rationale:
'The system prompt prevents discussion of any topic outside food and cooking. The 4+ rating reflects this restriction.'
How reviewers can test:
'A demo account with pre-loaded data is available: username reviewer@yourapp.com, password Demo1234. No API key is required — the app uses the app-managed key from the backend.'

If your Claude API key is embedded in the app (not recommended, but some apps do this for prototypes), reviewers will not be able to test without a valid key. Either build a reviewer-specific sandbox mode that returns pre-canned responses, or move the key to a backend.
API Key Architecture for Review
Never embed your Anthropic API key in the app binary. The preferred architecture for App Store apps routes all Claude calls through your own backend, which holds the key:
This architecture also lets your backend enforce rate limits, log calls for abuse detection, and rotate the API key without an app update — all of which reviewers view positively.
Output Filtering Before Display
Even with a tight system prompt, adding a lightweight client-side or server-side output filter provides defence-in-depth. A simple blocklist approach on the client:
A blocklist alone is insufficient as a primary safety mechanism — it will miss paraphrased content. Its value is as a safety net after the system prompt and Claude's own refusal training.
Common Rejection Reasons for AI Apps
No content reporting mechanism.
Even a single mailto: button satisfies guideline 1.2. Without it, rejection is near-certain for apps that generate open-ended text.
Age rating mismatch.
Submitting a 4+ app that can produce violence or profanity when prompted. Reviewers test adversarially.
API key inaccessible.
Reviewers cannot test AI features that require a key they do not have. Build a demo mode or provide reviewer credentials.
Inaccurate privacy nutrition labels.
Declaring no data collected when prompts are sent to a third-party API. Anthropic receives the text — declare User Content.
No AI disclosure label.
Presenting Claude's output as if authored by humans (e.g., 'Our experts say...'). Label AI-generated content visibly.
Summary

App Store compliance for a Claude-powered app boils down to five things: scope the system prompt to what the app is for, implement a content reporting path that satisfies guideline 1.2, assign an age rating that reflects the full capability of the model in your context, declare Claude-bound user text in Privacy Nutrition Labels, and add a visible AI-generated label to Claude's output. Explain all of this in App Review Notes — reviewers who understand the system before they test it are far less likely to trigger a rejection.
